structure.title: A key point to meet the challenges of developing automated driving functions, especially SAE J3016 automation level 3-5, is standardization: There is a huge potential in reducing development and validation costs by standardizing subsections of the complex hardware and software setup for automated driving functions. Sensors for detecting the vehicle environment and its connected environmental models are considered major enablers for automated driving. The current focus is on the standardization of sensor interfaces that serve as an input to environmental models or data fusion algorithms which in turn serve as the input for automated driving functions equal or greater than SAE level 3. Specifically, the focus is on interfaces for on-board sensors that can independently perceive their environment for performing safety critical tasks, e.g. camera, radar, lidar and ultrasonic sensors. This includes the actual measurement data as well as dynamically changing sensor performance values such as the currently estimated detection range. Other information sources like Car2X and maps are considered as potential extensions. With the resulting standardized sensor interfaces, OEMs, suppliers, service providers and tool providers can reduce their costs and time for the development and validation of automated driving functions.
structure.summary: Standardization of sensor interfaces is critical for reducing development and validation costs for automated driving functions (SAE levels 3-5), as it enables interoperability across OEMs, suppliers, and tools. Key focus areas include standardizing inputs for environmental models and data fusion, particularly for sensors like cameras, radar, lidar, and ultrasonic sensors, which provide dynamic data (e.g., detection range) and static parameters (e.g., mounting positions).

nodes.title: Intended audience
nodes.summary: The document explains the motivation, usage, and limitations of the Sensor interface standardization for autonomous driving on the AUTOSAR Adaptive platform. It targets users of the platform and professionals involved in sensor system development for autonomous vehicles. The interface serves as a critical link between stakeholders in future vehicle architectures. The text details different roles and their motivations, organized by key technical aspects of the standardization effort.

nodes.title: System Designer
nodes.summary: The System Designer perspective emphasizes the need for sensor standardization to support rapid prototyping. This requires a comparable and extendable interface that accommodates different sensor types. The goal is to ensure compatibility and scalability across diverse sensor implementations. The interface must enable seamless integration and adaptability during system development. Standardization in this context aims to streamline design processes while maintaining flexibility for future extensions.

nodes.title: System Engineer
nodes.summary: From the System Designer and System Engineer perspective, standardized communication protocols are reused to enable exchangeability and compatibility between sensors from different suppliers and the target sensor fusion platform. This approach ensures interoperability by leveraging common protocols, allowing seamless integration of heterogeneous sensor systems. The focus is on maintaining compatibility across diverse sensor sources while aligning with the requirements of the sensor fusion platform. Standardization reduces integration complexity and supports scalable system

nodes.title: System Integrator
nodes.summary: From the perspective of System Integrators and Application Developers, standardized interface and configuration descriptions are essential for enabling generic, testable software implementations and ensuring interoperability between systems. These descriptions allow for the creation of reusable components that support seamless integration. Application Developers rely on these standardized interfaces to efficiently implement fusion processes and algorithms. The interoperability and reusability are critical for achieving consistent and scalable system integration. The focus remains on maintaining technical accuracy without introducing external assumptions or details.

nodes.title: Safety and Security Engineer
nodes.summary: From the Safety and Security Engineer perspective, the interface must be compatible with standardized mechanisms to ensure adherence to established protocols. Validation of this compatibility is required during the development phase to confirm compliance and mitigate potential risks. The emphasis on standardized mechanisms and developmental validation underscores the necessity of integrating safety and security considerations early in the design process.

structure.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: The "Acronyms and Abbreviations" section provides definitions for terms relevant to the ara::adi specification. Key acronyms include AD (Automated Driving), ADI (Automated Driving Interfaces), AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking), HiL (Hardware

structure.title: Automated Driving Sensor Use Gases
structure.summary: The section outlines the main use case for automated driving sensor standardization, focusing on stakeholder perspectives. Chapter 3.1 provides a detailed description of the primary use case, while chapters 3.2–3.5 elaborate on specific stakeholder intentions and scope limitations. Use case 6 in chapter 3.6 expands the focus to virtual sensor simulation, extending the standardization scope. The text emphasizes structured detailing of stakeholder-specific intentions and scope constraints across different chapters. No additional technical details beyond these points are included.

nodes.title: Use Case 2: “Standardized Sensor API”
nodes.summary: The "Standardized Sensor API" use case enables OEMs to define sensor requirements for automated driving functions using ISO-standardized sensor information and an AUTOSAR-compliant interface. The scope focuses on standardizing the data format between smart sensors (e.g., tracking-integrated) and computing units (e.g., fusion modules). Constraints include relying on ISO for sensor signal interpretation and details, allowing API variations per sensor implementation, and ensuring a common interface for heterogeneous sensor technologies (LIDAR, RADAR, Camera). The example highlights redundant sensor integration (e.g., LIDAR, RADAR, Cameras) to meet automated driving safety and functionality needs. Sensor developers aim to leverage standardized programming APIs derived from system descriptions for consistent implementation.

nodes.title: Use Case 5: "Sensor Implementation Testing"
nodes.summary: The "Sensor Implementation Testing" use case emphasizes the need for standardized interfaces (e.g., ARXML) and detailed sensor data descriptions to enable testability at all integration levels. Sensor Implementation Testers require these specifications to validate automated driving functions, relying on AUTOSAR-provided test cases and standardized interfaces for interoperability. Testing involves evaluating fusion algorithms combining sensor signals, ensuring compatibility with Adaptive AUTOSAR ECUs. Additionally, Sensor Simulation Engineers use OSI and ISO 23150 specifications to simulate sensor fusion algorithms across virtual development stages (MiL, HiL), ensuring seamless integration into AUTOSAR Adaptive ECUs.

structure.title: Scope of Sensor Interface Standardization
structure.summary: The sensor interface standardization builds on the ISO specification, expanding it to fully comply with the AUTOSAR Adaptive platform by addressing syntactic implementation details and additional functionalities like sensor configuration. It excludes mechanical/electrical interfaces and raw data level interfaces, with raw data handling managed via the SWS Communication Management API. The main boundary condition is sensors connected via a service interface to the Adaptive computing unit, enabling direct connections to the ECU or data sharing between ECUs as illustrated in the figures. The scope includes examples of sensor setups and scenarios for inter-ECU data transmission, though specific hardware or raw data stream details are intentionally omitted.

structure.title: Sensor Interface API Design
structure.summary: The chapter provides an overview of the rationale behind the current Interface API design proposal for Smart Sensors. It outlines the design considerations and objectives aimed at enabling seamless integration and communication between smart sensors and external systems. The proposal emphasizes extensibility, modularity, and adherence to industry standards to ensure scalability and interoperability. Technical details include specifications for data formats, communication protocols, and error handling mechanisms. The design aims to balance flexibility with simplicity to support diverse sensor functionalities and deployment scenarios.

nodes.title: Sensor Interface realization as a Service
nodes.summary: The Sensor Interface is being realized as a Service to universally provide Sensor functionality across AUTOSAR platforms (Classic and Adaptive). This approach supports future sensor architectures depicted in the referenced figure. The figure, however, does not represent a valid scenario where a Classic platform can execute ADAS/AD applications. The decision emphasizes platform-agnostic Sensor service delivery. The service realization aims to align with evolving sensor integration requirements in automotive systems.

nodes.title: ISO mapping to Sensor Services
nodes.summary: The ISO standard defines Smart Sensors with object-level lists for tracking, roadmark, and landmark services, which are technology-agnostic. A separate sensor-technology-dependent feature list is used for detection. The Sensor Service design directly maps these ISO structures, ensuring each Smart Sensor provides tracking, roadmark, landmark services, and a detection service tailored to its specific technology. The figure illustrates this ISO list-to-Sensor Service mapping.

nodes.title: Sensor Service Template
nodes.summary: The Smart Sensor Service Template includes mandatory and optional elements, with optional components predefined at design time by providers and users. A capability vector indicates which optional elements are supported, while service profiles (e.g., Low/High) define varying performance levels using the same capability settings but different data resolutions. Compatibility between providers and users is verified during deployment or service discovery, with the latter involving a user requesting the capability vector to confirm required optional elements are supported. If met, the user subscribes to the service and processes data according to the capability vector's settings. Post-initialization, no further checks for optional elements occur.

nodes.title: Interface stability
nodes.summary: The interface stability in autonomous driving systems is challenged by the rapid evolution of sensors, which are critical for affordability and performance. High research and development efforts focus on improving sensors, accuracy, and sampling rates, leading to frequent technological advancements. These innovations make it difficult to establish long-term stable interfaces, as new capabilities and hardware requirements continuously emerge. The dynamic nature of sensor development complicates the design of interfaces that can remain consistent over time. This ongoing change necessitates adaptable solutions to maintain system compatibility and reliability.

nodes.title: Interface configuration
nodes.summary: The sensor interface configuration occurs at three distinct times: **design time**, where all connections are pre-specified, compiled, and flashed, potentially requiring sensor updates; **connection time**, allowing multiple interface versions for a connection, with a handshake to select the optimal version during system startup, ensuring safety validation; and **runtime**, enabling dynamic interface access but introducing complexity in safety analysis due to unpredictable configuration variants. Runtime is deemed unsuitable for autonomous systems due to safety risks and cost, while design time offers proven safety but lacks flexibility. Connection time is proposed as a balanced approach, enabling adaptability to evolving interfaces without compromising safety, as demonstrated by use cases like backward-compatible sensor updates and parallel interface testing. Future scenarios, such as multi-beam sensors adapting old interfaces, further highlight the necessity of connection time for seamless integration and system evolution.

structure.title: References
structure.summary: The "References" section cites ISO-23150, a standard specifying the logical interface for data communication between sensors and the data fusion unit in road vehicles, enabling automated driving functions. It defines the technical framework for interoperability and data exchange in sensor-to-data fusion unit interactions. The standard is critical for ensuring standardized communication protocols in automated driving systems. No additional references or details are provided in the section.
